name: Maple Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Maple. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for surface cleaning without charring

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for controlled wood ablation with minimal thermal damage

spotSize.description: Optimal beam diameter for precise cleaning control on wood surface

repetitionRate.description: Balanced repetition rate for efficient cleaning with controlled thermal input

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without wood damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient contaminant removal with minimal substrate heating

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete surface cleaning

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

power.description: Typical range for ns fiber lasers cleaning delicate organics like wood to avoid charring; starting at 100W balances efficiency and safety. Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics for 1064 nm systems; academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Wood Surfaces' (Journal of Cultural Heritage, 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal on wood; slower for heavy soiling (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on maple. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in industrial surface prep.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of organics; picosecond (ps) reduces heat-affected zone but is less common for wood. 50 ns minimizes thermal damage to maple's low-conductivity structure. Source: Laser physics research in 'Ultrashort Pulse Lasers for Material Processing' (Applied Physics Reviews, 2020).

repetition_rate.description: Standard for 1064 nm cleaning; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk heat accumulation on low-thermal-conductivity maple. 50 kHz recommended for balance. Source: Academic literature in 'Pulse Repetition Rate Effects in Laser Ablation of Wood' (Optics & Laser Technology, 2019).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe removal of contaminants from wood; maple damage threshold ~2-3 J/cm² due to charring onset. 1.5 J/cm² recommended below threshold. Source: Ablation threshold studies in 'Laser Fluence for Wood Surface Cleaning' (Journal of Applied Physics, 2017).

spot_size.description: Beam diameters for uniform coverage; smaller for fine details (100-200 μm), larger for broad areas (300-500 μm). 200 μm suits maple's surface texture. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems for industrial cleaning.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on maple without excessive exposure. Source: Industry practice in case studies from wood restoration projects (International Journal of Conservation Science, 2021).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform cleaning without gaps or over-ablation; 70% optimal for even coverage on irregular wood surfaces like maple. Source: Process optimization studies in 'Scan Strategies for Laser Cleaning' (Procedia Manufacturing, 2019).
